museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: January 2020: The year begins with PS5 and Xbox Series X known but not yet tangible.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for January 2020. Recent enough for careful source review before verification.

sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Dreams
items.body: Media Molecule releases a creation suite that feels like a gallery, a toy box and a workshop.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for February 14, 2020.

items.title: Ori and the Will of the Wisps
items.body: Moon Studios makes the late Xbox One era glow with craft.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 11, 2020.

items.title: Animal Crossing: New Horizons
items.body: Nintendo's island arrives exactly as real movement disappears.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 20, 2020; Axios covers its pandemic-era resonance.

items.title: Doom Eternal
items.body: Doom becomes the aggressive twin of Animal Crossing's lockdown weekend.
items.sourceNote: Gematsu/Bethesda delay context lists March 20, 2020.

items.title: Half-Life: Alyx
items.body: Valve makes VR feel, briefly, like the future PC players were promised.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 23, 2020.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII Remake
items.body: Square Enix turns a memory into a lavish, contested modern text.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for April 10, 2020.

items.title: The Last of Us Part II
items.body: Naughty Dog's sequel becomes a technical landmark and a bruising online argument.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for June 19, 2020.

items.title: Ghost of Tsushima
items.body: Sucker Punch gives PS4 one last sweeping open-world postcard.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for July 17, 2020.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: PlayStation 5 launches
items.body: Sony's new console reaches the UK and Europe on November 19 amid online-only launch-day ordering and stock pressure.
items.sourceNote: PlayStation Blog confirms the November rollout.

items.title: Xbox Series X/S launches
items.body: Microsoft launches two next-gen boxes on November 10, with Game Pass central to the pitch.
items.sourceNote: Xbox Wire confirms November 10, 2020.

items.title: COVID-19 reshapes events and retail
items.body: E3 is cancelled, showcases go online and physical shopping becomes unreliable or impossible for long stretches.
items.sourceNote: E3 2020 and COVID industry sources support this context.

items.title: Switch becomes a lockdown object
items.body: Animal Crossing, portability and family access make Switch hardware feel newly domestic.
items.sourceNote: Animal Crossing pandemic reporting and 2020 retail context support this reading.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Streams and servers
items.body: Online life becomes life: Discord calls, island visits, Zoom-adjacent hangouts and multiplayer routines fill spaces left by closed doors.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Services and stores
items.body: Digital showcases replace stage shows, making the industry feel both more accessible and more disembodied.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Forums and feeds
items.body: Stock-alert Twitter accounts, preorder queues and retailer crashes become part of buying a console.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Everyday connection
items.body: Patches, delays and remote development become visible to ordinary players in a way they rarely had before.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like games moved from hobby to infrastructure. They were where birthdays, friendships, arguments and boredom went during lockdown.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: Animal Crossing was not just a release; it was a place to stand when real places were unavailable.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: The new consoles arrived without the usual shop-floor theatre. For many people they were browser tabs, failed baskets and delivery windows.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: The year ended with wonder and fatigue sitting together: Hades, Flight Simulator and new hardware on one side, Cyberpunk, scarcity and exhaustion on the other.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
